item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That last sentence got me.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-100001 reminds me of SCP-1347.

Aside from that I really like how the realism is what makes this so scary.

item.title: My Birthday!!
item.content:encoded: I have been looking for a scip that has my birthday in it, and I finally found one! Hooray!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know, I never imagined finding a SCP centered on the Navier-Stokes equations. This is just great!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty neat. The only request that I would have is that you change the 'SCP-10001' to something else- just in case we someday do get to 10,001 SCPs.

item.title: Re: I just have one question:
item.content:encoded:
It depends on how specific the actual process is, but if it requires somewhere around 10^22 molecules (about enough to fill a shot glass) then it would take many times the age of the universe to actually achieve it through random motion.

ex: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Infinite_monkey_theorem

item.title: I just have one question:
item.content:encoded: Why, in all the years that the earth has been here, with its endlessly swirling oceans, hasn't this happened yet? Or is this what actually killed the dinosaurs or something.

item.title: Re: -1
item.content:encoded:
To be entirely fair, a lot of actual science is a very confusing way or saying "liquid goes boom" or "particle goes zoom". The intrigue of this SCP is in the pseudo-scientific details. It's still very technical for a piece of creative literature, but that is what gives it actual weight and intrigue.

In physics, the Navier-Stokes equations is one of the biggest unsolved problems that literal billions have been sunk into without an answer. They are viewed with a certain grudging mysteriousness due to how elusive they have proven to be. Even with all that, they are still just a complicated way of saying "liquid goes swoosh".
the described stirred cup of tea, if assumed to be 150 g, would have a yield of about 32.2 metric kilotons

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love love love everything except the ending. I speak just enough fluid dynamics/thermodynamics to get the concept and it's fantastic. buuuuuut…

+ collapsible for nitpicky grumbling about the ending
- grumble grumble

Something about pointing to a numbered SCP that doesn't exist, and will not exist any time soon, scratches me the wrong way, sure.

But having an inline specific description of that same SCP, outside of its article, that extremely casually mentions an object that would radically reshape any version of the Foundation ("How do we make more chains for 2317? How do you kill 682?" etc.) without dealing with any other consequences of that, is a bit of a dealbreaker for me on this.

In my mind, the bare minimum would be changing it to a SCP-████ or something along those lines, and changing the effect of that SCP to be a bit less drastic.

Alternatively, there's the option to link it back to an existing SCP. Random example: 946 displays a degree of omniscience, specifically related to unknown physics phenomena. It wouldn't immediately work with the nice endcap you've got, but there's plenty of other omniscient SCPs that this'd work with, too.

(I'd also dig a whole new canon/universe about the Foundation gaining the kind of perfect immediate omniscience you describe, what kind of anomalies would come out of that, what threats would emerge, which old threats would remain or change, etc. but that's probably a bit outside the scope of this one article lol.)

This is still totally a +1 for me though since my issue isn't really with the meat of the article. Love the concept, love the writing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As much as the ending makes this article, the implication that a being like SCP-10001 exists and is in Foundation custody raises a lot of questions. If such an SCP really existed, all of the unanswered questions they've accrued over the years could be solved easily.

It kind of seems like it removes the possibility for anything being unexplained, which is pretty sweet in-universe, but could potentially be limiting for other stories.

Perhaps you could expand on it a bit by formulating some kind of restriction?

novote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the concentration of energy includes the mass-energy of the fluid in the system.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is probably one of those articles where the science goes over my head, but I get the gist of it. I did find the punchline somewhat amusing, but I don't really think there's enough to the article for it to work. I understand a big point is the implication that something very simple can fuck with physics and be really dangerous, but it didn't strike me as more than a basic concept for a story. Considering the amount of dangerous SCPs that can easily go off (intentional or not) this would need to be the setup for a narrative rather than be the narrative itself.

-1

item.title: Extremely good.
item.content:encoded: I actually prefered the technical jargon, though superscript text could be added to help put readers with lower mathematical proficiency.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm kind of ambivalent about this one. What's there is excellent, but it feels like an introduction to a narrative that never appears. This is a very clever concept, and I think it's worth delving into more deeply.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1. Short but oh so sweet.
Now you just gotta figure out what to do when we reach 10000 scps :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Basic, efficient, and gorgeous in its subtle unsettling feel.

Good choice for the image too, I had my eye on that for a SCiP but it would seem that my plans have been foiled again!

+1

item.title: Re: -1
item.content:encoded: On the contrary this only works because of its short length. If this had been any longer, the punchline wouldn't have been enough to carry it. It get in, does its shit, and gets out.

item.title: Re: -1
item.content:encoded: That'd be fine if the implications weren't totally explicit. If it did something with the idea of easily producible explosives, then I could get behind this, but the length and bareness of the article works against whatever implications it was trying to set up.

item.title: Re: -1
item.content:encoded: The technical language works in its favor imo - and the scip is less about the flat idea of liquid go boom, its about the interesting implications of such an easily producable explosive.
This is a very confusing way of saying "liquid go boom".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Conversion of 1% of initial mass is ridiculously excessive, and to my knowledge isn't justified by the black hole. Unless you're claiming that the feedback loop concentrates all the mass together, which is a far cry from the vaguely plausible concentration of energy.

Regardless, +1 for the question about tea.

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded: That's not what I was thinking of, but it's at least as good as what I was thinking of. I was actually thinking of something quite a bit simpler.

Short and Sweet. I particularly liked the addendum about SCP-10001, it felt quite fitting.
Also, is the number 5772 a reference to the Reynolds number where Poiseuille Flow becomes unstable?

This is… quite interesting. It's neat how much it fits into such a small package with so few words, so my ADHD brain can read it without my eyes glossing over. Though the advanced terminology may require a few reads to fully comprehend. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This didn't work for me that well. I admire the thought put into its technical part, but the addendum shifts the tone too quickly and I'm not sure how exactly I was supposed to react to the reveal. There are some interesting implications to be drawn from that last sentence, but the way it was presented just didn't hit me.
The terminology slowed down the read a bit, however it was set up perfectly. The ending is extraordinarily fitting, kudos.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Big brain skip. As Rounder said, the amount of technical language was a bit of a trudge. The end is great.

+1.
I like it. Short and simple, but sweet. The addendum was awkward at first (SCP-10001 being an omniscient SCP) but the last lines sold it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A tentative upvote. It took me reading it three times while Captain Kirby yelled in my ear because my eyes kept glazing over from the technical language, but the ending makes it worth it. It's lean and intriguing enough to make it worth it.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Open problems in Mathematics are serious business.

Thank you for the feedback from Weryllium, stormbreath, Lt_Flops, Hampered Gibson and especially Captain Kirby.

Image is public domain, from https://www.olcf.ornl.gov/caar/frontier-caar/, Courtesy of Oak Ridge National Laboratory, U.S. Dept. of Energy.

If you can figure out the significance of the number, you win an internet cookie.
